**Hollywood**, sometimes informally called **Tinseltown**, is a [neighborhood](/wiki/List_of_districts_and_neighborhoods_in_Los_Angeles "List of districts and neighborhoods in Los Angeles") and district[[2]](#cite_note-2) in [Central Los Angeles](/wiki/Central_Los_Angeles "Central Los Angeles"), California. Its name has become synonymous with the [American film industry](/wiki/American_film_industry "American film industry") and the people associated with it. Many notable film studios such as [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures"), [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros."), [Walt Disney Studios](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(division) "Walt Disney Studios (division)"), and [Sony Pictures](/wiki/Sony_Pictures "Sony Pictures") are located in or near Hollywood.

Hollywood was incorporated as an independent [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") in 1903.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) The municipality of Hollywood was [consolidated](/wiki/Merger_(politics) "Merger (politics)") with the City of Los Angeles in 1910 following a referendum. Soon thereafter, the large portions of the American [film industry](/wiki/Film_industry "Film industry") migrated to the area.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Initial development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Initial development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/El_Cabrillo_Courtyard.png/250px-El_Cabrillo_Courtyard.png)](/wiki/File:El_Cabrillo_Courtyard.png)

[El Cabrillo](/wiki/El_Cabrillo "El Cabrillo"), a historic [Spanish Colonial Revival](/wiki/Spanish_Colonial_Revival_architecture "Spanish Colonial Revival architecture") landmark built in 1928 by [Cecil B. DeMille](/wiki/Cecil_B._DeMille "Cecil B. DeMille")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Glen-Holly-Hotel-1890.jpg/250px-Glen-Holly-Hotel-1890.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glen-Holly-Hotel-1890.jpg)

[Glen-Holly Hotel](/wiki/Glen-Holly_Hotel "Glen-Holly Hotel"), Hollywood's second hotel, at the corner of what is now Yucca Street, was built in the 1890s.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/HJWhitley_Hollywood_Hotel_ca_1904.jpg/250px-HJWhitley_Hollywood_Hotel_ca_1904.jpg)](/wiki/File:HJWhitley_Hollywood_Hotel_ca_1904.jpg)

[H. J. Whitley](/wiki/H._J._Whitley "H. J. Whitley") (on left wearing a [bowler hat](/wiki/Bowler_hat "Bowler hat")) and the [Hollywood Hotel](/wiki/Hollywood_Hotel "Hollywood Hotel") (on left) at the corner of [Highland Avenue](/wiki/Highland_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Highland Avenue (Los Angeles)") and [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Charles_Toberman_Estate%2C_Hollywood.JPG/250px-Charles_Toberman_Estate%2C_Hollywood.JPG)](/wiki/File:Charles_Toberman_Estate,_Hollywood.JPG)

[Villa Las Colinas](/wiki/C.E._Toberman_Estate "C.E. Toberman Estate"), a historic [Mission Revival](/wiki/Mission_Revival "Mission Revival") estate built by [Charles E. Toberman](/wiki/Charles_E._Toberman "Charles E. Toberman") in 1922

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Hollywood%26Highland-1907.jpg/250px-Hollywood%26Highland-1907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollywood%26Highland-1907.jpg)

The intersection of Hollywood and Highland in 1907

[H. J. Whitley](/wiki/H._J._Whitley "H. J. Whitley"), a real estate developer, arranged to buy the 480-acre (1.9 km2) E.C. Hurd ranch. Whitley shared his plans for the new town with General [Harrison Gray Otis](/wiki/Harrison_Gray_Otis_(publisher) "Harrison Gray Otis (publisher)"), publisher of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*, and Ivar Weid, a prominent [businessman](/wiki/Businessperson "Businessperson") in the area.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[Daeida Wilcox](/wiki/Daeida_Wilcox_Beveridge "Daeida Wilcox Beveridge"), who donated land to help in the development of Hollywood, learned of the name *Hollywood* from an acquaintance who owned an estate by that name in [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois").[[7]](#cite_note-7)
Wilcox is quoted as saying, "I chose the name Hollywood simply [because it sounds nice](/wiki/Phonaesthetics "Phonaesthetics") and because I'm [superstitious](/wiki/Superstitious "Superstitious") and [holly](/wiki/Holly "Holly") brings good luck."[[8]](#cite_note-8) She recommended the same name to her husband, [Harvey H. Wilcox](/wiki/Harvey_Henderson_Wilcox "Harvey Henderson Wilcox"), who had purchased 120 acres on February 1, 1887. It was not until August 1887 that Wilcox decided to use that name and filed with the [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County,_California "Los Angeles County, California") Recorder's office on a [deed](/wiki/Deed_(law) "Deed (law)") and [parcel](/wiki/Parcel "Parcel") map of the property.

By 1900, the region had a post office, newspaper, hotel, and two markets. Los Angeles, with a population of 102,479, lay 10 miles (16 km) east through the [vineyards](/wiki/Vineyard "Vineyard"), barley fields, and [citrus](/wiki/Citrus "Citrus") groves. A single-track [streetcar line](/wiki/Tram "Tram") ran down the middle of Prospect Avenue from it, but service was infrequent and the trip took two hours. The old citrus fruit-packing house was converted into a livery [stable](/wiki/Stable "Stable"), improving transportation for the inhabitants of Hollywood.

The [Hollywood Hotel](/wiki/Hollywood_Hotel "Hollywood Hotel") was opened in 1902 by Whitley, president of the Los Pacific Boulevard and Development Company. Having finally acquired the Hurd ranch and subdivided it, Whitley built the hotel to attract land buyers. Flanking the west side of [Highland Avenue](/wiki/Highland_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Highland Avenue (Los Angeles)"), the structure fronted on Prospect Avenue (later [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard")). Although it was still a dusty, [unpaved road](/wiki/Unpaved_road "Unpaved road"), it was regularly graded and graveled. The hotel became internationally known and was the center of the civic and social life and home of movie stars for many years.

Whitley's company developed and sold one of the early residential areas, the Ocean View Tract.[[9]](#cite_note-Los_Angeles-9) Whitley did much to promote the area. He paid thousands of dollars to install electricity and arrange for electric lighting, and he built both a bank and a road into the [Cahuenga Pass](/wiki/Cahuenga_Pass "Cahuenga Pass"). The lighting ran for several blocks down Prospect Avenue. Whitley's land was centered on [Highland Avenue](/wiki/Highland_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Highland Avenue (Los Angeles)").[[10]](#cite_note-A_Dream_Picture-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Why_I_live_in_Hollywood_by_H._J._Whitley-11) His 1918 development, [Whitley Heights](/wiki/Whitley_Heights,_Los_Angeles "Whitley Heights, Los Angeles"), was named for him.

### Incorporation and merger

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Incorporation and merger")]

Hollywood was incorporated as a [municipality](/wiki/Municipality "Municipality") on November 14, 1903, by a vote of 88 for and 77 against. On January 30, 1904, the voters in Hollywood decided, 113 to 96, to banish the sale of liquor within the city, except for medicinal purposes. Neither hotels nor restaurants were allowed to serve wine or liquor before or after meals.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In 1910, the city voted for a merger with Los Angeles in order to secure an adequate water supply and to gain access to the L.A. sewer system.

> With annexation, the name of Prospect Avenue was changed to Hollywood Boulevard and all the street numbers in the new district changed. For example, 100 Prospect Avenue, at Vermont Avenue, became 6400 Hollywood Boulevard; and 100 Cahuenga Boulevard, at Hollywood Boulevard, changed to 1700 Cahuenga Boulevard.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Motion picture industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Motion picture industry")]

Main article: [Cinema of the United States](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/NestorStudios-Hollywood-1913.jpg/250px-NestorStudios-Hollywood-1913.jpg)](/wiki/File:NestorStudios-Hollywood-1913.jpg)

[Nestor Studios](/wiki/Nestor_Film_Company "Nestor Film Company"), Hollywood's first movie studio, 1912

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/09/Hollywood-Studios-1922.jpg/250px-Hollywood-Studios-1922.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollywood-Studios-1922.jpg)

Hollywood movie studios in 1922

By 1912, major motion-picture companies had come West to set up production near or in Los Angeles.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In the early 1900s, most motion picture camera and equipment [patents](/wiki/Patent "Patent") were held by [Thomas Edison](/wiki/Thomas_Edison "Thomas Edison")'s [Motion Picture Patents Company](/wiki/Motion_Picture_Patents_Company "Motion Picture Patents Company") in [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), which often sued filmmakers to stop their productions. To escape this, filmmakers began moving to Los Angeles, where attempts to enforce Edison's patents were easier to evade.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Also, the weather was ideal for filmmaking and there was quick access to various settings. Los Angeles became the capital of the film industry in the United States.[[16]](#cite_note-Mintz,_S._2013-16) The mountains, plains and low land prices made Hollywood a good place to establish film studios.[[17]](#cite_note-Hayward,_Susan_2006._p._205-17)

Director [D. W. Griffith](/wiki/D._W._Griffith "D. W. Griffith") was the first to make a motion picture in Hollywood. His 17-minute short film *[In Old California](/wiki/In_Old_California_(1910_film) "In Old California (1910 film)")* (1910) was filmed for the [Biograph Company](/wiki/Biograph_Company "Biograph Company").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Although Hollywood banned movie theaters—of which it had none—before annexation that year, Los Angeles had no such restriction.[[21]](#cite_note-friedrich1986-21)

The first studio in Hollywood opened in early 1913, on Formosa Avenue down the street from Helen Muir's home.[[22]](#cite_note-Passion-22): 447 Her father [John Muir](/wiki/John_Muir "John Muir") returned from his tour of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [East Africa](/wiki/East_Africa "East Africa") a few months later and continued working on [Yosemite](/wiki/Yosemite_National_Park "Yosemite National Park") and his book *The Yosemite*.[[22]](#cite_note-Passion-22): 447 The [Nestor Film Company](/wiki/Nestor_Film_Company "Nestor Film Company") was the first studio, established in October 1911[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Hollywood,_Los_Angeles#Dubious "Talk:Hollywood, Los Angeles")*] by the New Jersey–based [Centaur Film Company](/wiki/Centaur_Film_Company "Centaur Film Company") in a [roadhouse](/wiki/Roadhouse_(facility) "Roadhouse (facility)") at 6121 [Sunset Boulevard](/wiki/Sunset_Boulevard "Sunset Boulevard") (the corner of [Gower](/wiki/Gower_Street_(Hollywood) "Gower Street (Hollywood)")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Four major film companies – [Paramount](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [Warner Bros.](/wiki/Warner_Bros. "Warner Bros."), [RKO](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures"), and [Columbia](/wiki/Columbia_Pictures "Columbia Pictures") – had studios in Hollywood, as did several minor companies and rental studios. In the 1920s, Hollywood was the fifth-largest industry in the nation.[[16]](#cite_note-Mintz,_S._2013-16)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] By the 1930s, Hollywood studios became fully [vertically integrated](/wiki/Vertical_integration "Vertical integration"), as production, distribution and exhibition was controlled by these companies, enabling Hollywood to produce 600 films per year.[[17]](#cite_note-Hayward,_Susan_2006._p._205-17)

Hollywood became known as *Tinseltown*[[23]](#cite_note-CollinsDictionary-23)
and the "dream factory"[[17]](#cite_note-Hayward,_Susan_2006._p._205-17) because of the glittering image of the movie industry.

### Further development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Further development")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Hollywood_boulevard_from_kodak_theatre.jpg/250px-Hollywood_boulevard_from_kodak_theatre.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollywood_boulevard_from_kodak_theatre.jpg)

[Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard") as seen from the [Dolby Theatre](/wiki/Dolby_Theatre "Dolby Theatre") in 2005

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Capitol_Records_Building_LA.jpg/250px-Capitol_Records_Building_LA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capitol_Records_Building_LA.jpg)

[Capitol Records Building](/wiki/Capitol_Records_Building "Capitol Records Building") in 1991

[A large sign reading *HOLLYWOODLAND*](/wiki/Hollywood_Sign#Origin "Hollywood Sign") was erected in the [Hollywood Hills](/wiki/Hollywood_Hills "Hollywood Hills") in 1923 to advertise real estate developers Woodruff's and Shoults' housing development. In 1949, the [Hollywood Chamber of Commerce](/wiki/Hollywood_Chamber_of_Commerce "Hollywood Chamber of Commerce") entered a contract with the City of Los Angeles to repair and rebuild the sign. The agreement stipulated that *LAND* be removed to spell *HOLLYWOOD* so the sign would now refer to the district, rather than the housing development.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

During the early 1950s, the [State of California](/wiki/Government_of_California "Government of California") constructed the [Hollywood Freeway](/wiki/Hollywood_Freeway "Hollywood Freeway") through the northeast corner of Hollywood.

The [Capitol Records Building](/wiki/Capitol_Records_Building "Capitol Records Building") on [Vine Street](/wiki/Vine_Street "Vine Street"), just north of Hollywood Boulevard, was built in 1956. The [Hollywood Walk of Fame](/wiki/Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame "Hollywood Walk of Fame") was created in 1958 as a tribute to artists and other significant contributors to the entertainment industry. The official opening was on February 8, 1960.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Martin1-27)

In June 1999, the Hollywood extension of the [Los Angeles Metro Rail](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Metro_Rail "Los Angeles Metro Rail") [Red Line](/wiki/B_Line_(Los_Angeles_Metro) "B Line (Los Angeles Metro)") [subway](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") opened from [Downtown Los Angeles](/wiki/Downtown_Los_Angeles "Downtown Los Angeles") to the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley"), with stops along Hollywood Boulevard at [Western Avenue](/wiki/Western_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Western Avenue (Los Angeles)") ([Hollywood/Western Metro station](/wiki/Hollywood/Western_(Los_Angeles_Metro_station) "Hollywood/Western (Los Angeles Metro station)")), [Vine Street](/wiki/Vine_Street "Vine Street") ([Hollywood/Vine Metro station](/wiki/Hollywood/Vine_(Los_Angeles_Metro_station) "Hollywood/Vine (Los Angeles Metro station)")), and [Highland Avenue](/wiki/Highland_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Highland Avenue (Los Angeles)") ([Hollywood/Highland Metro station](/wiki/Hollywood/Highland_(Los_Angeles_Metro_station) "Hollywood/Highland (Los Angeles Metro station)")).

The [Dolby Theatre](/wiki/Dolby_Theatre "Dolby Theatre"), which opened in 2001 as the Kodak Theatre at the [Hollywood & Highland Center](/wiki/Hollywood_%26_Highland_Center "Hollywood & Highland Center") mall, is the site of the annual [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") programs. The mall is located where the [Hollywood Hotel](/wiki/Hollywood_Hotel "Hollywood Hotel") once stood.

### Preservation and revitalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Preservation and revitalization")]

The [Hollywood Boulevard Commercial and Entertainment District](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard") was listed in the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1985.[[28]](#cite_note-nrhp-28) In 1994, [Hollywood, Alabama](/wiki/Hollywood,_Alabama "Hollywood, Alabama"), and ten other towns named Hollywood successfully fought an attempt by the [Hollywood Chamber of Commerce](/wiki/Hollywood_Chamber_of_Commerce "Hollywood Chamber of Commerce") to trademark the name and force same-named communities to pay royalties to it.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

After the neighborhood underwent years of serious decline in the 1980s, with crime, drugs and increasing poverty among some residents, many landmarks were threatened with demolition.[[30]](#cite_note-30) [Columbia Square](/wiki/Columbia_Square "Columbia Square"), at the northwest corner of [Sunset Boulevard](/wiki/Sunset_Boulevard "Sunset Boulevard") and [Gower Street](/wiki/Gower_Street,_Hollywood "Gower Street, Hollywood"), is part of the ongoing rebirth of Hollywood. The [Art Deco-style](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") studio complex, completed in 1938, was once the Hollywood headquarters for [CBS](/wiki/CBS "CBS"). It became home to a new generation of broadcasters when cable television networks [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV"), [Comedy Central](/wiki/Comedy_Central "Comedy Central"), [BET](/wiki/BET "BET") and [Spike TV](/wiki/Spike_TV "Spike TV") consolidated their offices there in 2014 as part of a $420 million office, residential and retail complex.[[31]](#cite_note-31) [Paramount Skydance Corporation](/wiki/Paramount_Skydance "Paramount Skydance") moved their corporate headquarters to Hollywood in August 2025 following the merger of [Skydance Media](/wiki/Skydance_Media "Skydance Media") and [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global").[[32]](#cite_note-HQ-32)

Since 2000, Hollywood has been increasingly [gentrified](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") due to revitalization by private enterprise and public planners.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) Over 1,200 hotel rooms have been added in the Hollywood area between 2001 and 2016. Four thousand new apartments and over thirty [low to mid-rise](/wiki/Low-rise "Low-rise") development projects were approved in 2019.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

### Secession movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Secession movement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Hollywood_Sign_%28Zuschnitt%29.jpg/250px-Hollywood_Sign_%28Zuschnitt%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hollywood_Sign_(Zuschnitt).jpg)

The iconic [Hollywood Sign](/wiki/Hollywood_Sign "Hollywood Sign") in the [Hollywood Hills](/wiki/Hollywood_Hills "Hollywood Hills")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/HollywoodSecesssion-Pro-5277.jpg/250px-HollywoodSecesssion-Pro-5277.jpg)](/wiki/File:HollywoodSecesssion-Pro-5277.jpg)

Mobile billboard promoting Hollywood secession from Los Angeles, October 2002

In 2002, some Hollywood voters[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] began a campaign for the area to secede from Los Angeles and become a separate municipality. In June of that year, the [Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County_Board_of_Supervisors "Los Angeles County Board of Supervisors") placed secession referendums for both Hollywood and the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley") on the ballot. To pass, they required the approval of a majority of voters in the proposed new municipality as well as a majority of voters in all of Los Angeles. In the November election, both measures failed by wide margins in the citywide vote.[[37]](#cite_note-DailyBruin2002-37)

### Decline of Film Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Decline of Film Industry")]

Following a peak in 2021 driven by the "[streaming wars](/wiki/Streaming_wars "Streaming wars")" resulting from the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), the [Hollywood film industry](/wiki/Hollywood_film_industry "Hollywood film industry") entered a period of contraction. By the mid-2020s, the region faced outsourcing to jurisdictions with more aggressive tax incentive, such as Georgia, the United Kingdom, and Ontario. Coupled with the long-term economic fallout from the [2023 Hollywood labor disputes](/wiki/2023_Hollywood_labor_disputes "2023 Hollywood labor disputes") and the high cost of living, on-location shoot days plummeted from 37,709 in 2021 to a record non-pandemic low of 19,694 by 2025, representing a nearly 48% decrease in local filming activity over four years.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

The decline has been particularly pronounced in scripted television and reality programming; according to **[Otis College of Art and Design](/wiki/Otis_College_of_Art_and_Design "Otis College of Art and Design"),** the number of shooting days in Los Angeles County in 2024 was down 40% compared to 2022 levels.[[41]](#cite_note-41) This downturn prompted the California State Legislature to pass Assembly Bill 132 in June 2025, which more than doubled the annual cap for the [California Film & Television Tax Credit Program](/wiki/California_Film_Commission "California Film Commission") to $750 million.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Despite the [merger](/wiki/Merger_of_Skydance_Media_and_Paramount_Global "Merger of Skydance Media and Paramount Global") of [Skydance Media](/wiki/Skydance_Media "Skydance Media") and [Paramount Global](/wiki/Paramount_Global "Paramount Global") in August 2025, which consolidated corporate headquarters in Hollywood, production remained suppressed as studios implemented ongoing spending cuts and shifted projects to lower-cost international hubs.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Geography")]

According to the [Mapping L.A.](/wiki/Mapping_L.A. "Mapping L.A.") project of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")*, Hollywood is flanked by the [Hollywood Hills](/wiki/Hollywood_Hills,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood Hills, Los Angeles") to the north, [Los Feliz](/wiki/Los_Feliz,_Los_Angeles "Los Feliz, Los Angeles") to the northeast, [East Hollywood](/wiki/East_Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "East Hollywood, Los Angeles") or Virgil Village to the east, [Larchmont](/wiki/Larchmont,_Los_Angeles "Larchmont, Los Angeles") and [Hancock Park](/wiki/Hancock_Park,_Los_Angeles "Hancock Park, Los Angeles") to the south, [Fairfax](/wiki/Fairfax,_Los_Angeles "Fairfax, Los Angeles") to the southwest, [West Hollywood](/wiki/West_Hollywood,_California "West Hollywood, California") to the west, and [Hollywood Hills West](/wiki/Hollywood_Hills_West,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood Hills West, Los Angeles") to the northwest.[[44]](#cite_note-MappingLACentralLA-44)

Street limits of the Hollywood neighborhood are: north, [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard") from [La Brea Avenue](/wiki/La_Brea_Avenue "La Brea Avenue") to the east boundary of [Wattles Garden Park](/wiki/Wattles_Mansion#Wattles_Park "Wattles Mansion") and [Franklin Avenue](/wiki/Franklin_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Franklin Avenue (Los Angeles)") between Bonita and Western avenues; east, [Western Avenue](/wiki/Western_Avenue_(Los_Angeles) "Western Avenue (Los Angeles)"); south, [Melrose Avenue](/wiki/Melrose_Avenue "Melrose Avenue"), and west, [La Brea Avenue](/wiki/La_Brea_Avenue "La Brea Avenue") or the West Hollywood [city line](/wiki/City_limits "City limits").[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45)[[46]](#cite_note-ThomasGuide-46)

In 1918, H. J. Whitley commissioned architect A. S. Barnes to design [Whitley Heights](/wiki/Whitley_Heights "Whitley Heights") as a Mediterranean-style village on the hills above Hollywood Boulevard. It became the first celebrity community.[[47]](#cite_note-Mr._Whitley's_Neighborhood-47)[[48]](#cite_note-HPOZ_Whitley_Heights-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Whitley_Heights_pg._34-49)

Other areas within Hollywood are [Franklin Village](/wiki/Franklin_Village "Franklin Village"), [Little Armenia](/wiki/Little_Armenia,_Los_Angeles "Little Armenia, Los Angeles"), [Spaulding Square](/wiki/Spaulding_Square,_Los_Angeles "Spaulding Square, Los Angeles"), [Thai Town](/wiki/Thai_Town,_Los_Angeles "Thai Town, Los Angeles"),[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45) and [Yucca Corridor](/wiki/Yucca_Corridor "Yucca Corridor").[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-ALook-51)

Like the rest of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), Hollywood has a [hot-summer Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Hot-summer_Mediterranean_climate "Hot-summer Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csa*) or dry-summer [subtropical climate](/wiki/Subtropical_climate "Subtropical climate"). Winters are typically mild and with light rain, but there are still plenty of warm, sunny days in the winter, as well. Summers are hot, sunny and dry, with virtually no rain falling between April and October; while summer days can be warm, they are considerably cooler than in the [San Fernando Valley](/wiki/San_Fernando_Valley "San Fernando Valley").[[52]](#cite_note-52) Spring and fall are generally warm, sunny, and pleasant. [Santa Ana winds](/wiki/Santa_Ana_winds "Santa Ana winds") typically occur during the fall and winter months, although they can occur during any month; Santa Ana winds bring heavy winds, higher temperatures and lower humidity levels, which increases the risk of [wildfires](/wiki/Wildfire "Wildfire"), especially in dry years. [Smog](/wiki/Smog "Smog") can sometimes occur during the summer months. May and June can be foggy and cloudy in Hollywood, a phenomenon known by southern California residents as "May Gray" or "[June Gloom](/wiki/June_Gloom "June Gloom")".[[53]](#cite_note-53)

The all-time record high temperature in Hollywood is 112 °F (44 °C) on June 26, 1990, and the all-time record low temperature is 24 °F (−4 °C) on both December 8, 1978, and December 23, 1990.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Demographics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Gateway_to_Hollywood_2018.jpg/250px-Gateway_to_Hollywood_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gateway_to_Hollywood_2018.jpg)

The *Four Ladies of Hollywood* sculpture on [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard") in 2018

The [2000 U.S. census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census") counted 77,818 residents in the 3.51-square-mile (9.1 km2) Hollywood neighborhood—an average of 22,193 people per square mile (8,569 people/km2), the seventh-[densest](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") neighborhood in all of [Los Angeles County](/wiki/Los_Angeles_County "Los Angeles County"). In 2008 the city estimated that the population had increased to 85,489. The median age for residents was 31, about the city's average.[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45)

Hollywood was said to be "highly diverse" when compared to the city at large. The ethnic breakdown in 2000 was 42.2% [Latino or Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), 41% [Non-Hispanic White](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_Whites "Non-Hispanic Whites"), 7.1% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_American "Asian American"), 5.2% [Black](/wiki/African_American "African American"), and 4.5% other.[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45) Mexico (21.3%) and Guatemala (13%) were the most common places of birth for the 53.8% of the residents who were born abroad, a figure that was considered high for the city as a whole.[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45)

The median household income in 2008 was $33,694, considered low for Los Angeles. The average household size of 2.1 people was also lower than the city norm. Renters occupied 92.4% of the housing units, and home- or apartment owners the rest.[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45)

The percentages of never-married men (55.1%), never-married women (39.8%) and widows (9.6%) were among the county's highest. There were 2,640 families headed by single parents, about average for Los Angeles.[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45)

In 2000, there were 2,828 military veterans, or 4.5%, a low rate for the city as a whole.[[45]](#cite_note-MappingLAHollywood-45)

## Radio and television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Radio and television")]

[KNX](/wiki/KNX_(AM) "KNX (AM)") was the last radio station to broadcast from Hollywood before it left [CBS Columbia Square](/wiki/CBS_Columbia_Square "CBS Columbia Square") for a studio in the [Miracle Mile](/wiki/Miracle_Mile,_Los_Angeles "Miracle Mile, Los Angeles") in 2005.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

On January 22, 1947, the first commercial television station west of the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"), [KTLA](/wiki/KTLA "KTLA"), began operating in Hollywood. In December of that year, *The Public Prosecutor* became the first network television series to be filmed in Hollywood. Television stations [KTLA](/wiki/KTLA "KTLA") and [KCET](/wiki/KCET "KCET"), both on Sunset Boulevard, are the last broadcasters (television or radio) with Hollywood addresses, but KCET has since sold its studios to the [Church of Scientology](/wiki/Church_of_Scientology "Church of Scientology") on Sunset, and plans to move to another location.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [KNBC](/wiki/KNBC "KNBC") moved in 1962 from the former [NBC Radio City Studios](/wiki/NBC_Radio_City_Studios "NBC Radio City Studios") at the northeast corner of Sunset Boulevard and Vine Street to [NBC Studios](/wiki/NBC_Studios_(New_York) "NBC Studios (New York)") in Burbank. [KTTV](/wiki/KTTV "KTTV") moved in 1996 from its former home at [Metromedia Square](/wiki/Metromedia_Square "Metromedia Square") on Sunset Boulevard to West Los Angeles, and [KCOP](/wiki/KCOP "KCOP") left its home on La Brea Avenue to join KTTV at the modern-day Fox Television Center. [KCBS-TV](/wiki/KCBS-TV "KCBS-TV") and [KCAL-TV](/wiki/KCAL-TV "KCAL-TV") moved from their longtime home at [CBS Columbia Square](/wiki/CBS_Columbia_Square "CBS Columbia Square") on Sunset Boulevard to a new facility at [CBS Studio Center](/wiki/CBS_Studio_Center "CBS Studio Center") in [Studio City](/wiki/Studio_City,_Los_Angeles "Studio City, Los Angeles").

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Emergency services")]

### Neighborhood councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Neighborhood councils")]

Hollywood is included within the Hollywood United Neighborhood Council (HUNC),[[65]](#cite_note-65) Hollywood Hills West Neighborhood Council,[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67) and the Hollywood Studio District Neighborhood Council.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69) Neighborhood Councils cast advisory votes on such issues as zoning, planning, and other community issues. ## Annual events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hollywood,_Los_Angeles&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Annual events")]

The [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), which honor the preceding year in film, have been held every year in late February/early March since 2004; prior to 2004, they were held in late March/early April. Since 2002, the Oscars have been held at the [Dolby Theatre (formerly Kodak Theatre)](/wiki/Dolby_Theatre "Dolby Theatre") at Hollywood Boulevard and Highland Avenue, with the exception of 2020, when due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") they were held in [Los Angeles Union Station](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Union_Station "Los Angeles Union Station").

The annual [Hollywood Christmas Parade](/wiki/Hollywood_Christmas_Parade "Hollywood Christmas Parade") goes down [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard") and is broadcast in the Los Angeles area on [KTLA](/wiki/KTLA "KTLA") and around the United States on Tribune-owned stations and the [WGN](/wiki/WGN_America "WGN America") superstation. The 75th edition of the parade was held in 2006.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

The [Hollywood Half Marathon](/wiki/Hollywood_Half_Marathon "Hollywood Half Marathon") began in 2012 and takes place every April. The event includes a Half Marathon, 10K, 5K, and Kids Fun Run along Hollywood Blvd., and is used to raise funds and awareness for local youth homeless shelters.
